title: Unable to run VS Code GUI through a Singularity container: Failed to load module "unity-gtk-module",  Failed to connect to socket /var/run/dbus/system_bus_socket, unable to create directory '/run/user/1000/dconf'
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.25.1-1531323788
- OS Version: Ubuntu 16.04
I am trying to avoid installing lots of dependencies of another software (Blender) on my laptop. Therefore, I decided to I build a Singularity container that has those dependencies so that I can compile Blender through that container. So my goal is to also have VS Code in the container, run it visually, compile Blender on my machine locally and do debugging/code editing etc. Here's how I build my container with VS Code installed in it:
```
BootStrap: docker
From: nvidia/cuda:9.0-devel-ubuntu16.04

%post

apt-get -y update && apt-get -y install locales
locale-gen en_US.UTF-8
apt-get -y update && apt-get -y install make \
                                        dpkg \
                                        wget \
                                        bzip2 \
                                        libglib2.0-0 \
                                        libxext6 \
                                        libsm6 \
                                        libxrender1 \
                                        g++ \
                                        gcc \
                                        xvfb \
                                        libyaml-cpp-dev \
                                        git \
                                        cmake \
                                        vim \
                                        curl \
                                        ca-certificates \
                                        software-properties-common \
                                        python3 \
                                        python3-pip \
                                        gdb \
                                        libopenexr-dev \
                                        zlib1g-dev \
                                        xserver-xorg \
                                        freeglut3-dev \
                                        mesa-utils \
                                        libreadline-dev \
                                        gnuplot \
                                        gnuplot-qt \
                                        unzip \
                                        libboost-all-dev \
                                        libnss3 \
                                        libgconf-2-4 \
                                        libasound2 \
                                        libsecret-1-0 \
                                        libnss3 \
                                        libgconf-2-4 \
                                        libasound2 \
                                        libatk-adaptor \
                                        libgail-common \
                                        libcanberra-gtk-module \
                                        gtk2-engines \
                                        gtk2-engines-*

apt-get -y install --reinstall overlay-scrollbar-gtk2 \
                                gtk2-engines-murrine

# Install Visual Studio Code and relevant extensions
cd /
mkdir vscode && cd vscode && wget -O code_amd64 https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=760868
dpkg -i code_amd64
code --install-extension ms-vscode.cpptools --user-data-dir='/vscode/'
code --install-extension vector-of-bool.cmake-tools --user-data-dir='/vscode/'
code --install-extension ms-python.python --user-data-dir='/vscode/'
rm code_amd64
```
After building the container I shell into it using the `--nv` flag. However, doing `code . -s` does not show Visual Studio Code GUI and shows a bunch of errors such as `Gtk-Message: Failed to load module "unity-gtk-module"`, `bus.cc(427)] Failed to connect to the bus: Failed to connect to socket /var/run/dbus/system_bus_socket: No such file or directory` and `dconf-CRITICAL **: unable to create directory '/run/user/1000/dconf': Permission denied.  dconf will not work properly.`. Need to mention that executing `glxgears` works as expected.
If I shell into my singularity container using `sudo` (which I should almost always avoid) and run `code -s --user-data-dir=''` I only get `ERROR:browser_main_loop.cc(279)] Gtk: cannot open display: :0` as error and the other warnings/errors disappear. `glxgrears` also shows a similar error: `Error: couldn't open display :0` despite the fact that `echo $DISPLAY` shows `:0` when shelling into the container both with and without sudo.
So I wonder, how can I properly run VS Code using Docker or Singularity? Doing some search on the Internet has sort of convinced me that I am installing all the necessary modules to be able to run an application with GUI in Singularity, but why is this not happening for VS Code? I hope VS Code developers can give me some clues on what's going on and either fix the issue or provide some solutions on how I can run the GUI when using a Singularity container.

title: Tree cheese
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.9.0
- OS Version: OSX 10.12.3
1. I cannot currently reliably reproduce this bug in any clean environment.
I am experiencing a bug concerning VSCode's tree view. Video of the bug is captured in [this Giphy link](http://www.giphy.com/gifs/26gss0EPsl58Yhj32).
In this video, I am simply clicking (to expand and contract) whichever folder is highlighted. You can see that the children of `templates` changes each time I expand and contract it.
```
FolderA
|-> FolderB
    |-> Content
```
If I have `FolderA` and `FolderB` expanded, then collapse `FolderA`, the next time I expand `FolderA` it displays some items from `Content` as `FolderA`'s child instead of an expanded `FolderB` with all of `Content` underneath it.
This is a rough description of what I am observing, but the behavior is inconsistent. As shown in the video, often it will choose different things from `Content` to render. I cannot reproduce the bug in a clean workspace. Quitting VSCode and restarting it tends to fix the issue, but it will begin to do it again after a couple hours of being open. When it happens, this behavior is not observed across all folders. There is nothing especially strange about the folders it happens in; they contain no more than a dozen direct children, they do not tend to be folders whose direct children have recently been edited, and the workspace itself has around 1000 files total.
I first noticed this bug after the update to 1.9.0.
I will attempt to capture more footage of it happening when it starts doing it again. Let me know if I can be of any other assistance!

title: Paste with syntax highlighting is not pasting empty lines
body:

title: Terminal Azure
body:
VS Code is ignoring Path env variable in Windows. I have already installed Azure CLI(beta version) and run "code ." in cmd to open VS Code. Then after writing az to access the CLI I got this message: az : The term 'az' is not recognized as the name of a cmdlet, function, script file, or operable program. Check the spelling of the name, or if a path was included, verify that the path is correct and try again. At line:1 char:1 In the same time I wrote az to cmd or powershell - az worked properly. +I had no VS Code running before installation +I tried to restart VS Code many times Solution: The cmd opened before installation caused the problem. Because spawned window of VS code from cmd took env variables from the terminal. It should only spawn the command explicitly, and not to spawn and pass implicitly background or env variables in simple example as "code ." title: During inertial scrolling on precision touchpad if Ctrl button is pressed, the font-size is increased of the editor
body: When I scroll though editor with precision touchpad and the editor window is still scrolling and then if I press the Ctrl button while it is still scrolling the font size increase or decreases depending on the scroll direction.

title: Python is not installed and The terminal shell path "cmd.exe" does not exist.
body:
Python is not installed and The terminal shell path "cmd.exe" does not exist. (+select python interpreter, as an addition to the first mistake)
This 2 errors appeared in my vscode...Could somebody help me, please. I am desperate…
First things first: I have already worked in python, using pycharm and in vscode. Then some mistake appeared. I red an advice, and, probably, made some incorrect thing in path. After everything stopped working. I have already tried: complete deletion python and vscode and reinstallation both of it; using anaconda; working with paths, made changes like / -> \\ -> and vice versa; When i installed python and the VScode editior, i checked the box on the first window asking to add to the path.
Nothing worked…
My paths:
The first one (for user):
C:/Users/name/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python38-32/;C:/Users/name/AppData/Local/Programs/Microsoft VS Code/bin; C:/Users/name/AppData/Local/Programs/Python/Python38-32/python.exe; C:/Users/name/AppData/Local/Programs/Microsoft VS Code/Code.exe; C:/Windows/System32/cmd.exe;
The second one (system):
C:/Users/name/AppData/Local/Programs/Microsoft VS Code/Code.exe; C:/Windows/System32/cmd.exe;C:/Users/name/AppData/Local/Programs/Microsoft VS Code/bin
I crave for all advices, you can share with!
Firstly, I would like to try to work with my paths again. For it, I ask smb, who also works in Windows, to copy his/her paths and send it to me. I will try to find mistakes or missing stuffs. If it is also isn't work…Well, I have no idea. I will thankfully read all your advises. Looking forward for your answers. Thanks in advance.

title: Extension dev/debug: using vscode.workspace.findFiles

title: VSCode installed inside a container is not shown, but Firefox and Android Studio are shown
body:
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes
- VS Code Version: 1.59.0 (but does not matter)
- OS Version: Ubuntu or Debian
1- Install VSCode and Firefox inside a docker container using this `Dockerfile`:
```
FROM ubuntu:latest

WORKDIR /Temp

RUN apt-get update

RUN apt install wget -y

RUN wget https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=760868 -O vscode.deb

RUN ls

RUN apt install ./vscode.deb -y

RUN apt install libxshmfence1 -y

RUN apt install libasound2 -y

RUN apt install firefox -y

#CMD [ "code", "--user-data-dir='~/.vscode-root" ]
CMD ["/usr/bin/firefox"]
```
2- Build it using this command:
`docker build -t ubuntu-vscode .`
3- Temporarily give permission to `xhost`:
`xhost +`
4- Run the container using this command:
`docker run -it --net=host --env="DISPLAY" --volume="$HOME/.Xauthority:/root/.Xauthority:rw" ubuntu-vscode`
5- You can see that Firefox is now showing in your local computer, but it's run inside the docker container.
6- Now switch the last `CMD` command in the docker file, rebuild the image, and re-run the container. You see that VS Code is run, but we see nothing.

title: Better defaults for files.trimTrailingWhitespace
body:
Using vscode for TypeScript with the default setting I've found I get all kinds of trailing spaces appearing.
At least in one case (markdown) end of line whitespace is important to allow, though. Not sure what kind of complexity that adds.
Basically, this issue boils down to **it's nice when the defaults 'just work'.**
However, this might be something for https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-editorconfig to handle instead. According to the vscode-editorconfig repo [README.md](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-editorconfig/blob/ff3f2f13f8ea50ac78b39ecf13d0c4b434c9f743/README.md) as of now `trim_trailing_whitespace` is "On the backlog".

title: (feature request) Scroll Binding
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.8.1
- OS Version: Windows 10 AU
Please, refer to [Vim Scroll Binding](http://vimdoc.sourceforge.net/htmldoc/scroll.html#scroll-binding):
> Occasionally, it is desirable to bind two or more windows together such that
> when one window is scrolled, the other windows are also scrolled.  In Vim,
> windows can be given this behavior by setting the (window-specific)
> 'scrollbind' option.  When a window that has 'scrollbind' set is scrolled, all
> other 'scrollbind' windows are scrolled the same amount, if possible.  The
> behavior of 'scrollbind' can be modified by the 'scrollopt' option.
Its really nice with with 2 VSCode panels, side by side, we can have that option too.

title: Keybinding: Allow colon to be a valid keybinding
body:
Working on a [VIM extension](https://github.com/VSCodeVim/Vim). A core key binding of VIM is `:`.
The below is a workaround:
```
     {
        "key": "Shift+;",
        "command": "extension.vimMode_colon",
        "when": "editorTextFocus"
      },
```
but it doesn't work on international keyboards.

title: VSCode API in tests: not all commands have visible effect during debugging (async)
"Open file in editor" from terminal opens a new split, instead of changing focus to a different one
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.49.2
- OS Version: Windows 10 Enterprise
1. Have multiple "splits" (I think this is what VS Code calls them, like different files open at the same time. Basically different tabs)
2. Have some file `left.cpp` open on the left split and `right.cpp` open on the right split.
3. In the terminal, run `echo "left.cpp:4"` (some string that VS Code recognizes as a path to a file that it allows you to control-click to open file in editor)
4. With focus on `right.cpp`, ctrl-click on that link.
The result is that we open a _new_ tab in the right split to refer to `left.cpp`. Even though `left.cpp` is already open and visible in the left split.
This is mildly annoying, and would be much nicer if the editor first checked if `left.cpp` was visible in any of the splits -- and if it was, navigate in that split (regardless of which split currently has focus).
I wouldn't call this a _bug_ per se, since `left.cpp` does successfully get opened. More of a improvement request.
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: When I tried to check this:
```
$ code --disable-extensions .
Ignoring option disable-extensions: not supported for code.
```

title: Feature flag/setting for Panels in Activity Bar buttons UI
body:
The new UI to switch to a particular panel is a good step forward, but it needs more cooking and at the same time we want to get feedback. Therefore add a setting for enabling the new UI, the setting should be off by default.
The plan is that we announce the feature in the release notes and invite users to provide feedback.

title: GitHub credentials aren't saved when prompted from vs code
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.12
- OS Version: Win10
- Git Version: 2.11.1 then 2.12.2
This was first reported to https://github.com/Microsoft/Git-Credential-Manager-for-Windows/issues/421 but is being cross posted here since it's unclear if the issue is coming from VS Code or the Git Credential Manager.
What's happening is the GCM keeps prompting me to login to GitHub and even when I do the credentials aren't saved to the Windows Credential Manager. I can repeatedly enter them and I'll continue to be prompted to login. Even hitting cancel no longer fully dismisses the prompt because in about 60 seconds it comes back. If I enter my login info to the GCM via the command prompt then they're stored and I'm no longer prompted when opening Code, but if I'm prompted from in Code then they aren't saved.

title: While importing, resolve file named as directory instead of "index"
body:
I have a project setup where I'm using `directory-named-webpack-plugin`.
So, the imports are
```
import Header from 'components/Header';
```
Where the `components` directory contains `Header` directory which contains `Header.jsx`
Though I can configure `paths` in `jsconfig.json` for VSCode to resolve this absolute path, peeking or `Go To Definition` still does not work unless I do:
```
import Header from 'components/Header/Header';
```
Which means that VSCode import resolver looks for `index.jsx`, instead of `Header.jsx`.
Is there any existing way to tweak this behaviour?

title: "Generate Commit Message" subsequent clicks should generate a different message
body:
1. Have some changes
2. Click on "Generate Commit Message"
3. Get some response. Now imagine that the user is not happy, and clicks "Generate Commit Message" again
4. I get the same message 🐛
I think LLMs usually return multiple responses. Can we make sure that on subsequent clicks of Generate Commit Message we show the alternative? So basically sub-sequent clicks are "regenarate".
I know this might be conflicting with https://github.com/microsoft/vscode-copilot-release/issues/632
And I think the flow in this issue is more important.

title: Opening strings such as template literals in a new buffer, synced with original
body:
There are many issues asking for language support in embedded strings, such as using HTML in ES6 template literals. It seems that it will take considerable effort to get done, and are not yet on the roadmap as far as I know.
As an intermediate solution, could VS Code facilitate opening embedded strings (when in TypeScript, for example) on the side buffer and syncing it with the original string, so that one could use any language infrastructure that is already automatically in place today for that buffer (highlighting HTML, SQL, etc)? Copying strings across buffers is of course possible already (and works great!) but it is not really usable until selecting the string and keeping it in sync is mostly automatic. Maybe they could be "portals" or something. Alternatively one could use some kind of narrowing to the part in question, but it is imperative that language services would work properly for the narrowed part as they do now for a separate buffer.
**Later edit**: Published an extension that implements this, works great: [Template Literal Editor for VS Code](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=plievone.vscode-template-literal-editor). Will try to remember to keep it updated when Extension API offers new possibilities (scroll and cursor sync, document tiles, etc).

title: Bug in "detecting an armstrong number in c++ for no.153" program
DocumentSymbolProvider does not work with VirtualDocuments
body:
1. Register a custom DocumentSymbolProvider for the language of your choice in this case we chose python (we tried with scheme: 'our custome scheme' and without). 2. body:
I compiled the VSCode as described [here](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/wiki/How-to-Contribute#build-and-run-from-source). After that I have created the `code` script with the following content:
```
(cd /some/path/vscode/src/scripts && ./code.sh)
```
and made it executable:
```
chmod +x /usr/local/bin/code
```
After that when I run the VSCode, hit Ctrl-Shift-p and type `ext install` I see the 'No extensions found' message, please see the attached file.

title: Allow searching the Change Language Mode screen by associated file extensions
body:
For example, if the list of languages is being displayed and you want to change the language to `C#`:
![](https://puu.sh/w0Epy/e70ef4c535.png)
It would be great if you could search for it by file extension `cs` and have it show up in the results.
![](https://puu.sh/w0EDx/35c51d23af.png)
_Side note: `cs` currently doesn't match `csharp`, even though it is displayed in parentheses next to the language name. Is this a bug/oversight?_
Similarly, being able to search for Markdown with `md`, YAML with `yml`, and C++ with `cpp` would be great.

title: [shaderlab] Support Shaderlab Highlighting for .cginc (Include files)
body: Unity Cg Include files are essentially Shaderlab files that are pasted in when the shader is compiled. Right now highlighting for them is not supported in VS Code, the fix is to simply add ".cginc" to the package.json file in the shaderlab folder.

I think there should be a "Toggle Sidebar" in the View menu.

title: Have Click to Follow Link tooltip label contain the URL
body:
I'd like to suggest an improvement where _Click to Follow Link_ tooltip label would also show the URL the link leads to. There is basic linkification in VS Code for textual URLs which themselves show you the target, but we also have `DocumentLinkProvider`s which can underline arbitrary ranges. I think it would be useful to show the URL these lead to.
These are the files that I think need to be updated to make this possible:
[`src/vs/editor/contrib/links/links.ts:29`](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/blob/master/src/vs/editor/contrib/links/links.ts#L29)
Also line 41. I am not sure how the dynamic URL would be appended to the string. The only use of `HOVER_MESSAGE_GENERAL_META` seems to be at line 57 and AFAICT there is no context from which the URL could be pulled.
[`src/vs/workbench/parts/debug/browser/linkDetector.ts:37`](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/blob/master/src/vs/workbench/parts/debug/browser/linkDetector.ts#L37)
This file may also need to be updated, but I think this only handles converting textual URLs to clickable URLs, not contributed link ranges provided by `DocumentLinkProvider`s from extensions, which is my main interest.
[`src/vs/workbench/parts/terminal/electron-browser/terminalLinkHandler.ts:186`](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/blob/master/src/vs/workbench/parts/terminal/electron-browser/terminalLinkHandler.ts#L186)
This is for the context of the integrated terminal (AFAICT). I don't think `DocumentLinkProvider`s contribute to the Terminal, so this should be just plan `file`/`http`/`https` links being converted to clickable ones. It's actually probably not too useful to show the URL in the tooltip here as you can see it, maybe only if it normalized and expanded `file` URLs so the user doesn't have to infer the path.
I'd like to PR this if this is deemed a worthwhile inclusion, but I'd like to get some pointers on how I could get the actual URL for the ranges highlighted by `DocumentLinkProvider`s. Also would like to hear others opinion on whether to do just there ranges or all URLs, even linkified plain text links where it seems a little redundant because you can already see (some form) of the URL.

title: ShowKeyboardWidget should have an option to disable it other than `readOnly`
body:
When the editor is used on a mobile browser, it shows a keyboard widget. It seems the only way to disable this is to set `EditorOption.readOnly`.
https://github.com/microsoft/vscode/blob/b8cace23ff07521a17df6c07ecb3becdb6db8d46/src/vs/editor/standalone/browser/iPadShowKeyboard/iPadShowKeyboard.ts#L33
Can there be an option to explicitly disable this widget without having to specify `readOnly`? Usually, tapping on the editor is enough to display the keyboard, making the widget redundant. It would be helpful if it could be hidden without being in `readOnly` mode (opt-out basis).
Note: Also filed an issue on the [monaco-editor](https://github.com/microsoft/monaco-editor/issues/4822) repo.

title: Reveal arbitrary files/folders in sidebar
body:

I often face the situation where I want to find a folder or file but only in sidebar but without opening it immediately (e.g. when searching for DB migration files wich often have not a name I remember). As the workspace grows and many folders are opened in the tree, it becomes hard to find a specific folder.
So maybe a panel very similar to the one which appears with Ctrl+E (quick open) would be very helpful like "quick reveal", which allows to search for an arbitrary path an reveals it in the sidebar.

title: Keeping open file history
body:
So, one thing I really liked about 1.2 and prior, is files did not belong to an "editor" (pane). It was one of my favorite things about vscode. I can open and close editors when ever I want. Sometimes something is not relevant or I want more room for one file, so I will close other editors. Keeping files and editors separate lets you move around your codebase without worrying about losing history. I thought this was a bug at first, since I have both tabs and "open editors" hidden in the UI as I don't need or use them.
Via twitter I heard these new split editor features were wildly requested. Feature requests are easy to make, but its hard to vote on _keeping_ a feature that you already like.
My suggestion would be to keep the awesome work you did with tabs, but maybe store all open files in a single list also. So any time you open a file, it gets pushed to that list, while also being associated to and editor for tabs and what not.
Thoughts?
- VSCode Version:
1.3
OSX

title: Don't auto-scroll to first "Find" match
body:
-- Edit --
To anyone arriving here, please continue discussion at #60977, which has more traction and is thus likelier to be voted into being a feature.  Note, though, that this thread addresses some subtleties of the Find Widget behavior that aren't mentioned there, so for anyone who wants to be extra comprehensive, please see the post with the 3 cases and accompanying GIFs below.  Thanks!
-- end Edit --
Hello, I'm not sure if this should be considered a bug or a feature request, but it is something I run into probably 10-20 times per day.
Dear reader, see concrete example at bottom, if that's more your thing.
**What happens now?**
I am often looking for instances of a string of characters on my current screen, near my current position.  So, I open the "Find" widget (Cmd+F | Ctrl+F) --  **but, as I begin typing what I'm looking for the editor immediately scrolls to the first match of whatever I've typed so far, even if that's up at the top of the document.**  Even when I finish typing what I was looking for, I then need to scroll back to wherever I was working, which is a big break of focus, especially on a long file.
**What would you like instead?**
So, I'd like "Find" to highlight what's typed in the "Find" widget, but not actually jump to the first match until Enter is pressed.  That way a user can use Find for visual aid on the current screen.  This could be an option in settings, or even default behavior, since you're almost certain to need to press Enter anyway to go through subsequent "Find" matches.
**Why can't I use "Search" instead?**
I want to see the string I'm searching for in the context of the current page, not in a list of matches.
I usually have the sidebar hidden to save screen space.
**Current workaround**
I can type the string I'm looking for in the main body of the editor, select it, and then let the editor  highlight other instances -- but obviously this isn't ideal, since the user shouldn't be typing non-desired code into the coding area just to look for nearby code.
**Concrete example**: want to see nearby instances of string `xkcd`.
(This is obviously simplified, just to make the point.)
xkca
xkca
xkca
xkca
xkca
xkca
xkca
xkca <-- current cursor position
xkca
xkca
xkcd
xkca
xkca
xkca
xkca
**Upon typing "xkc", editor scrolls to top to document, highlighting "xkc", but what I actually wanted was to finish typing "d" so that I could easily see the match nearby.  When I do press "d", editor scrolls to "xkcd", but not at the same screen position as I was originally.**

title: Opening function definition will not go to already open file if file in split panel.
body:
I have VS Code 1.38.0.
I'm programming in C and I like working with files open side by side. Let's say I have a file "A" open on the left side and a file "B" open on the right side. If there is a function call in file A that is defined in file B, if I right-click on the function call and select "Go to Definition", a new tab with the file B will open on the left side and now I have file B open twice. What i want instead is for the already open file B to just go to the definition of the function. Is there any setting to enable that?
This behavior actually works when you have one panel only and multiple tabs but doesn't work if you have both left and right panel open.

title: easier way to make vscode use the entire workspace for code hints for javascript [accessability]
body:
i'm told there's a way to configure vscode to use my entire workspace for providing code hints for javascript. but i can't figure out how to do that.
i'm not using any compiled javascript packages/libraries/whatever like Vue or React or Node.js
i have a parent.js file with a parent class and a child.js with a child class. and i'm including both of those files into an html page with references. (a little more complex than just two files but you get the point)
i'd like to be able to work on child.js without having to open parent.js without having to completely redo the entire project using some heavy library/framework like Vue or React that i've never used before.
if vscode really does already support this. the documentation really doesn't explain how. all of the config.json docs heavely reference "compiler" and "module" and expect that i'm doing some sort of export and import. which i have no idea what how those are supposed to work in javascript. i'm certainly not using them for my project.
it shouldn't be this hard to just get vscode to use my entire workspace for code suggestions. i can understand that you might want to also limit it to using parts of the workspace... or to define different javascript groups or whatever.
but i just need it to use the entire workspace. i'd much rather it gives me hints for js files that aren't getting included in the final html page than have to constantly go digging through my project to find the file with the class definition i need.
i really don't want to have to flatten a bunch of files  i've organized into folders to make them easier to find into one massive file that i have to use ctrl-f to find the class definitions.
it shouldn't be this hard. if it really is possible to use jsconfig.json to make vscode use the entire workspace can somebody please provide an example of what this setting should look like. because the sample code i found in the docs doesn't work.
```json
{
    "compilerOptions": {
        "module": "commonjs",
        "target": "es6"
    },
    "include": ["js/**/*"]
}
```
i've tried several different things for the include path. what am i doing wrong?
again. this shouldn't be this hard. it really should just be a simple check box in the settings. or really... it should be the default way it works. with limiting which files it uses should be the thing that requires complicated settings. also doing anything with settings in vscode is such a nightmare.

title: Explorer refresh issue again
body:
Reviving https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/issues/19377
It's possible I'm misunderstand the design. If the explorer is intended to stay in sync and does so, why have a refresh button? Saw the discussion at https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/issues/17354#issuecomment-267524776.
- VSCode Version: Version 1.12.0 (1.12.0) (running OSS Dev)
- OS Version: OSX 10.11.5 (15F34)
1. Open up the vscode repository.
2. Open `node_modues` folder in explorer and navigate to around `rcedit` to see the proper location.
3. Toggle internal terminal and run npm install `react-grid-layout --save`
4. Notice that `react-grid-layout` has not showed up.
5. Click the refresh button in top right of explorer. Notice that react-grid-layout now appears. Incidentally, refresh is a jarring experience and you lose your place in the editor.
I built it from source (is there a live or hot reload functionality? or at least an automatic restart? didn't seem to have it) and dropped a few console logs in the explorerView.ts file. It seems to run `shouldRefreshFromEvent()` when I add and delete; noticed it getting as far as https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode/blob/a9c372aa55243b8c6e181c337504861df54bba66/src/vs/workbench/parts/files/browser/views/explorerView.ts#L556.
Happy to do some more investigating if nececcary but I'd prefer to get livereload or even the debugger working first...

title: Symbol icons could be so much more helpful
body:
I've been using VS Code extensively for more than a year now, and I must say I still don't know the meaning of symbol icons:
![VS_icons](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1711539/78226262-bf023f00-74cb-11ea-9b1c-00d951238bf0.png)
IE, the only useful info that these icons convey me is that some symbols are of different kind.
In contrast, IntelliJ's much simpler icons have a very clear meaning, even if you haven't used IntelliJ a lot:
![IJ_icons](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1711539/78226255-bdd11200-74cb-11ea-94cc-857c2e4db6dd.png)
`f` is field, `C` is class, `m` is method, `E` is enum, etc...
I wish VS Code used such letter mnemonic scheme as well, it seems more useful for new and expert users alike.
https://www.jetbrains.com/help/idea/symbols.html#
https://www.jetbrains.com/help/clion/icons-and-symbols.html#

title: Allow extensions to change font for source code in editor
body: It would be really cool to be able to customize the display of my source code a little more generally than just colorization. The ability to apply CSS to editor elements would be fantastic, but individual preferences would be fine with me. As an example of what I mean, this would enable re-styling comments to be in a hand-written font, or have calls to certain functions be extra large. Hopefully this request makes sense, I think it could unlock some really powerful feedback mechanisms, but maybe I'm barking up the wrong tree. Would love to know either way. Thanks VSCode team!

title: Ctrl + Down doesn't keep cursor in viewport
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.13.1
- OS Version: Windows 10
Those who are used to Visual Studio will be disoriented with this behavior. When you're using ctrl + down to move in the file, the cursor position will stay the same even when it goes out of view. So when you press any of the arrow keys after the scroll, the viewport will jarringly jump to the initial cursor's position.
The behavior we're used to in Visual Studio will keep the cursor in the viewport whenever possible (the cursor would stick to the first line in the viewport while going down), which I believe is the better behavior. Can't there be at least an option to switch between the 2 behaviors?

title: Use HTML automatic code formatting method for SVG files
body:
- VSCode Version:
```
  Version 1.12.1
  Commit f6868fce3eeb16663840eb82123369dec6077a9b
  Date 2017-05-04T21:40:39.245Z
  Shell 1.6.6
  Renderer 56.0.2924.87
  Node 7.4.0
```
- OS Version: Ubuntu 17.04
1. Open SVG file with improper formatting (no line breaks, bad tabbing and spacing, etc.)
2. Press Ctrl-Alt-I (in Ubuntu) or the similar commands for other OSes (see list below)
**Expected:**
Since SVG is essentially the same as HTML, it should be formatted in a similar way to HTML. The keyboard shortcuts should work with SVG files.
**Workaround:**
- Rename the SVG file to `[Filename].html`
- Open the file in the editor
- Press the appropriate keyboard shortcut to format the "HTML"
- Rename the file back to `[Filename].svg`
**Shortcuts for various OSes:**
VSCode on Windows - Shift + Alt + F
VSCode on MacOS - Shift + Alt + F
VSCode on Ubuntu - Ctrl + Alt + I

title: toLocaleString displaying time 00:00:00 as 24:00:00
body:
- VS Code Version: 1.53.2 (user setup)
- OS Version: Windows_NT x64 10.0.18363
1. Toggle Developer Tools in any VSCode editor
2. Go to Console tab, and enter `new Date(1612760400000).toLocaleString(undefined, {timeZone: 'America/New_York', hour12: false})`
3. Console will return "2/8/2021, 24:00:00" instead of "2/8/2021, 00:00:00"
Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes
The issue occurs in Developer Tools as well as any extensions that use the `toLocaleString` function. It can be reproduced with any timestamp that corresponds to times between 00:00:00 and 00:59:59. It only appears to occur when the `hour12: false` option is used--otherwise, the time correctly displays as 12:00:00AM.
This issue does not occur in pure JavaScript, nor in other TypeScript implementations (tested on https://www.typescriptlang.org/play)

title: Infer / complete variable names from the type
body:
In Java you write: `Type variableName`. Eclipse for example infers the `variableName` from the `Type`, because the `Type` is written before the `variableName`.
In Typescript you write: `variableName: Type`. If the programmer begins to write the `variableName` VSCode should look in the available types to infer the completion for the `variableName`.

title: File is a CommonJS module; it may be converted to an ES6 module.
body: I know this issue was recently closed, but they locked the thread and the final answer provided by @mjbvz is incorrect.  He stated that you must **add "javascript.suggestionActions.enabled": false to your user settings**, but it needs to be added to your WORKSPACE settings, not user settings. ```json
   {
     "key": "f2",
     "command": "-editor.action.rename",
     "when": "editorHasRenameProvider && editorTextFocus && !editorReadonly"
   }
   ```
2. Add the following shortcuts from my custom configuration:
```json
   {
     "key": "f2",
     "command": "workbench.action.focusActiveEditorGroup",
     "when": "explorerFocus"
   },
   {
     "key": "f2",
     "command": "workbench.view.explorer",
     "when": "editorFocus"
   }
   ```
3. With these shortcuts, the F2 key should allow quickly switching focus between the explorer and the editor, which used to work fine before the January 2021 update. Now, switching from the editor to the explorer works, but then hitting F2 does not do anything and does not switch back to the active editor group as it should considering the configured shortcuts.

title: Clear recent files without clearing recent projects
body:
A big project I am working on recently renamed some directories, so many "recently opened" files now do not exist any more. Vscode does not seem to realize this until I actually try to open each individual one of them, which means that the first attempt to open any file will now fail for a few weeks or so, until they are all gone. (So maybe it would be good if it could just prune non-existing files but that's not what this issue is about.)
I'd love to just "clear recently opened files", but when I do this, it also deletes all my recently opened project folders, so Ctrl-R becomes useless. That's even more annoying than having to open every file twice. It would be great if vsocde wouldn't let me choose between a rock and a hard place here, and just delete the recently opened files of the current project without affecting the global list of projects that I have opened recently.

title: Copy path from navigation bar
body:
Being able to copy the path (either relative or full) from the navigation bar at the top of the editor would be pretty useful. For example, right-click on the file or folder name and select `Copy Path` or `Copy Relative Path`, much like the options that are available when right-clicking on a file or folder in the explorer pane.
Here's an extremely professional mockup in paint
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/58235000/143584147-067da641-2cc1-416c-8ccd-0f2875f05169.png)
An example scenario where I'd find this useful is running commands against against a specific file, for example, running a single test suite from the command line.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/58235000/143584752-b0363553-6512-410b-8db5-ce3c5f4e7692.png)
Rather than having to type the path to the file (or having to expand the side-bar which, I tend to have closed when working with split editor), it would be quite useful just to click the file from the navigation bar and select `Copy Relative Path` and paste it into the terminal.

title: Git Commit All for single directory.
body:
It would be really nice to be able to be in the explorer, right click a directory and create a git commit for just the changes in that directory.
The specific scenario I'm thinking about is with WordPress, where you might have your entire deployment in a repo. I do this for local testing to make sure that everything works and then I deploy the updated plugins.
I may at the same time update WordPress core if I feel like there is some issue with a plugin update that may be resolved by having the latest core.
So you have this giant commit of all of these changes you made. While it might not be important. I think it would be nice to have tooling that allows you to easily do commits for individual directories. That way if you want to commit plugin updates for individual plugins as separate named commits you can.
The built-in git I didn't dig into very far but it seems to just want to do your whole working space and not encourage much in the way of clean commits.
I'm sure I can find another way to do this, but since I like vscode and it seems like a good feature I thought I would mention it. :)

title: API to open document in a non-persistent tab
body:
I am creating an extension to interact with reverse engineering tools like IDA Pro and Hopper. My extension will provide a list of functions in the sidebar, and when clicked they will open a document of the disassembly or pseudocode for that function.
I would like it to behave the same way the file explorer does: when clicked, a "non-persistent" tab is opened. And when a new function is clicked, it replaces the old non-persistent tab with its own contents, etc. Currently, calling `window.openTextDocument()` opens a persistent document.

title: What is the correct behavior of running Code 0.10.10 from the command line?
body:
As requested by @bpasero in #4006, I'm opening an issue on this.
Using 0.10.10, when I run Code from the command line without any options specified, it always reuses the existing window. To get a new window, I have to specify the `-n` option.
Based on the `--help` output, this seems to be the expected behavior, but @bpasero seemed to think that it was unusual.
I have tested this on two different Windows machines and a Mac, and the behavior is the same in all cases.
I don't have any custom user settings on any of the installations, so I don't think anything is interfering there.
As far as repro steps go it's pretty simple:
1. Make sure Code isn't running
2. Open a Command Prompt / PowerShell window / bash terminal
3. Type `code` at the prompt
4. Repeat this several times and see that the focus goes to the existing window and no new windows are opened.
5. Type `code -n` at the prompt and a new window will open.
Is this the expected behavior for 0.10.10, or is something wrong?

title: allow changing of relative line number starting number
body:
I use the VSCodeVim extension and have `editor.lineNumbers` to `relative` in order to easily figure out how many lines I want to change using Vim commands.
The issue is that the `relative` setting is 0-indexed, which means I always need to add 1 to the relative line number, which is easy but not optimal.
Can we have a new setting, named `editor.relativeLineNumbersStartingIndex` or something like that, that you can set to the number that the current line is considered. So if it's set to 0, it will work like it does today, and if it's set to 1, it would work something like this:
[current line number]

title: Terminal Auto Reply: `Terminal will be reused by tasks, press any key to close it.`
body:
Can Terminal Auto Reply handle the the exit message from VS-Code Task spawned terminal?
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25647368/158283041-0bdcab99-4fbf-4eee-ba20-c40c583319f0.png)
Ultimately I just want to auto reply to message on the task end (preferably without having to edit task setting of the project for whole team).
Expected this would work, but it does not (first one is triggered properly, but the task exit message doesn't seem to work):
```json
  "terminal.integrated.autoReplies": {
    "Terminate batch job (Y/N)?": "Y\r",
    "Terminal will be reused by tasks, press any key to close it.": "\r"
  }
```
Unclear if this is:
- Auto Reply only being triggered on first found instance (this scenario has two required responses)
- Task terminal exit message not actually being in the terminal, thus no reply
- Not specific enough message - does the message need wildcard e.g `*press any key to close it*`
- No idea if wildcards are supported, only documentation I found on this (https://code.visualstudio.com/updates/v1_64#_automatic-replies) doesn't seem to indicate wildcard support
Workaround:
https://stackoverflow.com/a/67872135/7650275
Configure `Task` -> `Presentation` -> `Close` setting to true: `      "presentation": {"reveal": "always", "close": true}`
**Downside:** Outputs error message to VS Code alert:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/25647368/158283524-84d04e4e-9a9f-41bb-a858-f9e0915bf2e2.png)
CC: @Tyriar @meganrogge possible triage/quick close

title: Suggestion: semantic highlighting
body:
It would be great if VS Code supported the paradigm of semantic highlighting.
In short, it makes syntax highlighting more useful, by varying the colors of the same syntax-types of different names in a given scope. This makes it easier to catch mistakes and to see the flow of variables (since every variable in a given scope has a unique color, yet based of the same color range).
Here's an in depth explanation of what is is with some examples: https://medium.com/@evnbr/coding-in-color-3a6db2743a1e#.n2zb9ohw8
Example - before:
![Before](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1760/1*CTbkJuYm0JUcHFTAgovAsg.png)
Example - after:
![After](https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/max/1760/1*TVSPOYO1z8GOVs3tuxNRqA.png)

title: Windows: F1 should open some kind of help and not command pallette
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.2.1
- OS Version: Windows 10
My keyboard has the word **Help** stamped right on the **F1** key's legend.   As a long-term Windows and Visual Studio user, I expect **F1** to mean Help!   Preferably context-sensitive help that focuses on what I'm focused on (DWIM.)  So for example, when I'm editing README.md, I sort of hope that F1 to produce a Markdown cheat sheet.
While I appreciate the command pallet that F1 currently invokes in VSCode, it can be invoked by other means (e.g. **Ctrl+Shift+P**), and some interesting expectations seem to have come from mapping F1 to the command pallet.   #7269.
I couldn't agree more that an F key shortcut is justified, perhaps **F4** would be a better mapping for displaying the command pallet given the traditional Windows UI guidelines.   **F4** has long been used in contexts such as a combo-box to display a list of alternatives.   See [Guidelines for Keyboard User Interface Design](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms971323.aspx).   Also try, for example, pressing **F4** in the windows explorer, internet explorer, etc.

title: A horizontally scrolling column view
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.11.1
- OS Version: Windows 10
**An editor view that ONLY has a horizontal scroll bar. Text is layed out in columns which are automatically sized to be as wide as the widest text in that column.**
Motivation: Like many programmers I have a gigantic screen, but my ability to see a lot of a single source code file is limited by the vertical height of my screen, meaning I often use less than a 3rd of my screen real-estate. The proposed view would allow me to see much more of my file at once. Opening multiple views on the same file are harder to manage.
This is a feature request, since it doesn't seem possible to add this functionality as a plugin. Alternatively, consider adding enough functionality to the plugin API to make this feature possible.
Optionally: intelligent pagination. Inserting new lines would shift all columns, which may be disorienting. Instead, use the natural indentation of most text files to move text to the next column when the "page break" would be nested more deeply than a configurable amount of spaces. This would ensure most "functions" would be in a single column (not divided), and code would move around between columns much less while editing.
Example, current view, `*` marks the edge of the editor view:
* code                   S*
*   code                 C*
* code                   R*
* code                   L*
*   code                 L*
New proposed view, `.` marks unused space because of pagination:
* code     code     code  *
*   code     code         *
* code       code         *
*          code           *
* S C R O L L B A R       *

title: please add a better way to force focus back to the editor
body:
One problem with VSCode and screen readers is this: there seems to be no quick way to return focus to the editor after switching views.
1. Open a few files. Be sure you're in the editor for a file.
2. Press ctrl-shift-f to do a global search.
3. Try to get out of that and back to your editor. Since global find is a view, and the editor isn't a view but is always visible (I think), there's no keystroke/command to put you back in the editor.
I usually press ctrl-1, but if you use multiple groups, you have to know which group you want. Ctrl-k, e is the other option, but that puts you in a list, not straight back into the most recent editor.
It seems like there should be an easy way to get out of global search, debug, and the other views and back into the active editor. Yes, ctrl-k then e will bring up a list of editors with the most recent at the top, but you then have to press enter. Ctrl-{1-0} will work, but you have to know which group you want. The solutions I can come up with:
1. Offer a setting that allows the user to choose the behavior of ctrl-k, e. Either go to the most recent editor, or offer the list as happens now. Remapping this command and changing the setting would thus offer a single keystroke method for getting into the editor.
2. Offer a new command, and clarify the existing one so it is obvious that it will go to a list and not directly back to the editor.

title: Make API to open a new window of VSCode
body:
Hi everybody.
I haven't found a feature like mine so I am opening it.
# Description
I need a possibility to open a new window from my code.
I would like to have a method like this:
```typescript
export namespace vscode {
    /**
     * Opens a new window using the specified path as a target.
     * @param path An entry located at the path is opened in the new window. If it is undefined the new window is opened without target
     */
    export function createWindow(path: string | undefined): void;
}
```
And use it like this:
```typescript
const newWindowWithFile = vscode.createWindow('/etc/passwd');
const newWindowWithDirectory = vscode.createWindow('/mnt');
const newWindow = vscode.createWindow();
```
# Unresolved Questions
Should the method return `Promise` to schedule actions after a new window is opened?
Should the method return `Promise` to return an error if a new window isn't opened?
Regards,
Alexey

title: Test UI: Right-click context menu action executes against wrong item when multiple tests selected
body:
Version: 1.60.0-insider (Universal)
Commit: 7c25c174726ab969db3f99058cdf24bcfae1cfbe
Date: 2021-08-11T05:13:17.497Z
Electron: 13.1.8
Chrome: 91.0.4472.164
Node.js: 14.16.0
V8: 9.1.269.39-electron.0
OS: Darwin arm64 20.5.0
1. Create extension that contributes a command to `testing/item/context`
2. Populate test UI with 3 tests and select all 3 tests (i.e. multi-select)
3. Right-click on the bottom item and run the contributed command
**Expected Outcome:**
Either:
(a) the command is executed for all test items, or
(b) the command is executed for the item where I right-clicked
**Actual Outcome:**
The command appears to be executed only once, for the first test that appears in the Test Explorer (not the test that I right-clicked).
I like the idea of being able to run a command for multiple selected items (i.e. behavior (a)). I also think it would be ideal if there was a `context key` that I could use to know that multiple items are selected so that I can show/hide commands based on whether command should run in "single" or "multiple" context.

title: Test UI: Ability to contribute to context menu commands
body:
As a VS Code API developer, I would like to be able to contribute to the VS Code context menu commands in the test UI (e.g. see `Hide Test` shown below):
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2074089/129125571-d985d27e-a99a-4ef7-ad18-76bfc455b86b.png)
Ideally I want to be able to show/hide commands for each individual TestItem. Some examples of menu items we would want to contribute:
* Jump to Error Source
* Add to Exclusive Test Run
* Add to Filter
* Remove from Filter
I don't imagine the contribution points/menu names would be fixed across all API contributors. For example, I can imagine some other more complex scenarios, for example `Open last CI Run Result`.

title: Go To Definition with the cursor inside the target definition triggers Go To References instead
body:
We're implementing a custom language server and encountered the following issue:
When using Go To Definition and the position is already inside the definition we would go to, i.e. when something inside the definition refers to itself, VS Code will not jump to the beginning of the definition and highlight it, as it would usually do, but instead will act as if Go To References was used.
I'm assuming this is not intentional, since I don't see why it would act like that. I would expect it to jump to the beginning of the definition and highlight it, even when we're currently inside that range.
To reproduce the issue, I've also tried it with a publicly available language server, specifically the Python extension by Microsoft.
```python
class Foo:
    def foo(self):
        x = Foo()

class Bar:
    def bar(self):
        y = Foo()
```
When using Go To Definition on "Foo" in "y = Foo()", it works as expected. When using it on "x = Foo()", it takes a few seconds and then pops up the references dialog, same as when Go To References is used.

Testing #29144
I don't think we should hide or mask the fact that the extension writes to user settings.
> New features are available for the Sublime Text Keymap 3.0. Do you want to enable the new features?
Perhaps "Would you like to customize VS Code to behave more like Sublime Text?"
> Sublime Text Keymap: New features have been added.
Perhaps "User settings have been successfully modified" with a link to "Review user settings".
The error case (e.g. dirty or invalid `settings.json`) should present the error message.
> Sublime Text Keymap: New features have not been enable.
Do not show this prompt, unless an error case occurred.

Does this issue occur when all extensions are disabled?: Yes
- VS Code Version: 1.64.0
- OS Version: MacOs BigSur 11.6.1
1. Clone the [repo](https://github.com/nickImbirev/vscode-editor-title-command-bug) with a bug sample
2. Run the `npm install` command
3. Press F5 button (to start the debugging process)
4. Open the folder in the Explorer panel with some files inside (it will be enough to open the repo folder in it)
5. Open two (or more) files in the opened folder (i.e. README.md and package.json)
6. Click the `Hello-world` button in the right top corner of the active editor to emulate the bug
**AS IS:** the user will see a notification with amount of visible editors (it will be only 1 editor)
**TO BE:** the user should see a notification with a **proper** amount of visible editors (it should be the same, as the amount of editors they opened in the step 5)

title: Allow modification of the "file path detection" in the terminal
body:
When running a Dart/Flutter project in VS Code's integrated terminal, one often sees stack traces using Dart's import syntax
```
[...]
#4      UserArea.build.<anonymous closure> (package:aym/pages/user_area/user_area_widget.dart:58:51)
[...]
```
VS Code identifies `package:aym/pages/user_area/user_area_widget.dart:58:51` as a path, but when CMD clicking it, the modal says "No matching results".
In this case I have to go to the left and remote the `package:` (which is Dart's default package-level import prefix), as well as the `aym/` (which is the prefix for the current project I am in).
Do you think it'd be valuable for VS Code to support modifying this behavior, or should this be better taken up with the stack-trace-printing project to use (full/relative) file paths?

title: [Search] Allow to use "\t" as a replacement for a tabs or similar

Currently there is no easy way to find tabs in your program code. Find a amount of spaces is much easier.
![2020-09-16 14_17_43-●](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/17874713/93335969-721ae680-f827-11ea-9be6-53acaae1f9e2.png)

title: Font choice
body: [Issue](url) Type: Feature Request A monospaced font with a slashed zero would be nice especially one that does not look like there are spaces after i's and n's (Monaco). I also like the exaggerated parens, braces and brackets, too. I prefer small font sizes and with most fonts differentiating between the above 3 is not easy. one, lowercase L and upper case I and vertical bar | can also be difficult to tell apart. highlighting paired quotes double and single would also be nice. I found the highlighting matching brackets, (parens and braces too?) Font ligatures are nice too. However secondary to the above considerations. Lastly a suggestion select mechanism that allows a tab to be ONLY a tab also not using the enter key would be nice. title: Silence the startup command printout in Integrated Terminal
body:
When I run a Dart app in the Integrated Terminal, it prints out a very verbose startup command, e.g.:
`cd /Users/mikem/myproject; /usr/local/Cellar/dart/2.8.2/libexec/bin/dart --enable-vm-service=0 --pause_isolates_on_start=true --write-service-info=file:///var/folders/2n/vn59_vz152vg107rrs53ympc0000gn/T/dart-vm-service-ee74.json -DSILENT_OBSERVATORY=true --enable-asserts bin/main.dart`
This happens for Javascript too:
`mikem@MBP node_sample %  /usr/local/bin/node --inspect-brk=6018 scripts/simple.js
Debugger listening on ws://127.0.0.1:6018/453abe68-1da3-4862-8ba4-71d8e3dfd8d6
For help, see: https://nodejs.org/en/docs/inspector
Debugger attached.`
These printouts pollute the scrollback and aren't very useful imho. Could we get an option to silence this output, so the first thing I see in the Integrated Terminal is one of the print statements actually in my code?
Note that this doesn't happen when using the Debug Console. There, I only see this printout (which also would be nice to silence, but at least it's less verbose):
`Connecting to VM Service at ws://127.0.0.1:58210/4yciVJ1NsWE=/w`

title: Add a default shortcut for duplicating line
body:
tl;dr: Add a default shortcut for duplicate line that is close to existing shortcuts in popular text editors.
- Textmate, Atom and Sublime have the shortcut `Cmd-Shift-D`. Brackets has `Cmd-D`
- This specific shortcut is highlighted in a recent [CSS Tricks](https://css-tricks.com/on-switching-code-editors/) article as important custom shortcut.
- Anecdotally, it is also the only custom entry I added in my shortcuts even though I have used VS Code for years.
As `Cmd-Shift-D` is taken, would `Cmd-D` be a good stand-in and close enough to most users' muscle memory?

title: VSCode URI scheme
body:
It would be awesome if VSCode can support URI scheme:
```ts
code://path/to/file?line=1col=2
```
So if you click on any link on your computer it can open a file on VSCode and position the cursor on the correct line and column.
I used this on sublime https://github.com/asuth/subl-handler before together with SASS debug info before sourcemaps was supported to jump back to Sublime Text for debugging CSS.
There is still some good use cases, except for the outdated SASS debug info. I for instance want to run:
```ts
tsc -w --pretty --uri-scheme code
```
And see errors and be able to jump back to VSCode to correct my code.
There are similar URI schemes developed for TextMate and Emcas:
https://github.com/purcell/emacs-handler

title: Proposal: Change order of completions of CSS properties.
body:
When I write css properties, sometimes I feel annoyed at the order of completion.
For example, I type `borr` and Tab with the intentention of being converted to `border-radius:`,
but it is converted to `border-bottom-left-radius:`.
I think it's better to be converted to `border-radius` in this case.
I think this is because completions are ordered by natural order of property name.
So, I think it's better for completions to be sorted by `(numberOfChunksSeparatedByHyphen, nameOfProperty)`.
By this,
border-bottom-left-radius -> (4, "border-bottom-left-radius")
border-radius -> (2, "border-radius")
so, border-radius is given priority,
and in other cases, shorter properties are given priority.

title: Hover shows different type than associated type definition (reached via Go to Definition)
body:
1. Paste the following JavaScript snippet into an editor (if necessary, change the language to JavaScript):
```javascript
   const element = document.querySelector('a')
   element.getAttribute('test')
   //      ^^^^^^^^^^^^ hover here.
   ```
2. Hover over `getAttribute`. The return type of the `Element.getAttribute` method is shown as `string`.
3. Activate the “Go to Definition” command for `getAttribute`. This opens [TypeScript’s `lib/lib.dom.d.ts` file](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/blob/main/lib/lib.dom.d.ts#L4908-L4911). The return type of the `Element.getAttribute` method is shown as `string | null`.
**Expected outcome**:
The return type of `Element.getAttribute` on hover is shown as `string | null`.
**Actual outcome**:
The return type of `Element.getAttribute` on hover is shown as `string`.
**Comments**:
- There is likely a good explanation for why this is the case that I’m unaware of, but writing some basic JavaScript code and taking a return type as shown VS Code for granted could lead me to unexpected errors in my program if I didn’t know that `Element.prototype.getAttribute` can in fact return `null` when `qualifiedName` can’t be resolved to an existing attribute on the `Element`. I expect the types on hover and when going to the type definition to be the same.
- The same thing happens with `/** @type {HTMLElement} */ (element).dataset.test` where `test` is shown to be a `string` when it can be `undefined` as well (according to the interface `DOMStringMap` and according to testing it in Firefox).
VS Code version: Code 1.66.2 (dfd34e8260c270da74b5c2d86d61aee4b6d56977, 2022-04-11T07:49:24.808Z)
OS version: Linux x64 5.13.0-39-generic snap
Restricted Mode: No
Extensions: none (ran VS Code via `code --disable-extensions`)

title: Setup challenges when running mocha tests
body:
## Goals
- Since I run tests many times, I want to run mocha tests out of the box using a keyboard shortcut or a flat command (i.e. one that doesn't require input).
- I want to navigate directly to test suites and test cases.
- I want to jump to the last failed test of the most recent test run with a keyboard shortcut.
## Getting to a keyboard shortcut
- Add a `test` script to `package.json` and install the `npm` extension. Then I can use <kbd>Cmd+R T</kbd> to run the npm test script.  Somehow I have to learn that the npm extension can be helpful.
- I can try to use tasks. I need to configure tasks, need to configure the default test task, and I need to assign a keyboard shortcut to the `run test task` command. That's three big hurdles. We have a tasks menu, but that's only talking about build tasks not test tasks. Steep learning curve.
## Issue with the terminal
- In both cases (npm and tasks) the test execution happens in a terminal. The terminal is reused.  When running tests I care about the failing tests. The terminal gives me link detection to where the test failed. Great. When I do this the terminal is scrolled away from its default position, i.e. the most recent line of output. Thus, when click the link and I fix the issue and re-run the tests then I don't see the output of the re-run. I need to go to the terminal and scroll to the bottom to see if the test succeeded or failed. A terminal that shows tests should scroll automatically to the bottom.
- I can assign a keyboard shortcut to focus the active terminal (there is none by default). However, in order to find the failed tests and the link to the source I use the mouse. I'd like to jump to failing tests directly with a keyboard shortcut.
## Navigation issues
- Show Document Symbols and Show Workspace Symbols are unaware of mocha test suites and test cases. I always use search to navigate to my tests.
## Issues when debugging
- The debug console does not support link detection. Thus, when I run tests in the debugger without hitting a breakpoint, I can not easily navigate the test failure.

title: When a TypeScript file is focused, show the TypeScript version number on the bottom status bar
body:
# What
When a TypeScript file is focused, VS Code shows "TypeScript" in the status bar:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5a9e90fc-695d-454b-9ed4-7c12b3f8e802)
My feature request is to show the TypeScript **version number** here, too, so the status bar looks like this:
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/64fc8e01-7d0e-44d1-b6c6-91fa94b15961)
# Why
- The TypeScript language moves fast and sometimes I want to know if I'm behind.
- The status bar is the expected place to find that information. Since it's not there, I click on it like a button, but it pops up options that don't seem to lead to the version number either.
- The best way I've found to get this information is to:
1. Open up a terminal and type `npx tsc -v`.
2. Check the package.json or package-lock.json file. The former can be inconclusive or misleading if it uses semvar versions.
All of these solutions feel like I'm going outside the IDE to gain information (I assume) it already has.
- I'm not even sure if those three solutions are guaranteed to be the same version VSCode uses. Maybe VSCode comes with its own compiler it can fall back on, etc. Maybe my package file(s) say one thing but my node_modules directory contains a different version. What does VSCode do in that scenario? (This is a rhetorical question)
- Having the IDE tell me the TypeScript version helps me rule out certain possibilities when troubleshooting TypeScript issues.
- Even if the TypeScript version number is 1 click away (if it is, I don't know where to find it), I think showing it on the status bar would improve the user experience.

title: Command line argument documentation and a '--help' option
body:
Hello!
Visual Studio Code seems to accept command line arguments for some things, such as disabling extentions and openning to a given path, however they do not seem to be documented anywhere (that I can easily find at least). I'm sure many other users have run into the problem of wanting to know what vscode supports when opened from a terminal and the first thing most of them will try is passing the `--help` option to vscode.
So, my feature request is for a standard help/usage message to be displayed when giving the `--help` option to vscode.

title: How to setup a development environment to debug an extension that uses local changes to vscode.d.ts?
body:
I would like to experiment with some changes to vscode.d.ts (the API exposed to extensions).
In order to test these changes locally, I would like to be able to have an extension FOO such that
1. FOO can consume the new/modified APIs in vscode.d.ts
2. I can set breakpoints in FOO and VS Code for debugging.
In my case FOO=vscode-go (an extension that already exists).
***tl;dr*** what is the recommended setup to accomplish what I want?
Long version...here is what I have attempted...
**Method 1:** Inline vscode-go into `vscode/extensions/vscode-go`.
This requires making quite a few changes in vscode
- Exclude vscode-go from some lint rules in gulpfile.hygine.js
- Add vscode-go to list of extensions in build/npm/postinstall.js
- Update vscode-go's package.json
- Remove vscode as a dev dependency from vscode-go
- Remove references to `node ./node_modules/vscode/bin/install`
- Update compile and watch scripts to look like `gulp compile-extension:vscode-go` and `gulp watch-extension:vscode-go`
- Add a refs.d.ts to point to local version of vscode.d.ts
```
    /// <reference path='../../../../src/vs/vscode.d.ts'/>
    /// <reference path='../../../../src/vs/vscode.proposed.d.ts'/> 
    ```
- Extension specific hacking. For vscode-go this involved
- Updating main entry point due to how extension compilation works
```
        -"main": "./out/src/goMain",
        +"main": "./out/goMain",
        ```
- Rewriting some import paths
```
        -import { getBinPathFromEnvVar } from '../src/goPath';
       +import { getBinPathFromEnvVar } from './goPath';
        ```
- Export interfaces that need to be exported
- Update settings in tsconfig.json
I had this method working for a bit (both debugging and consuming new APIs), but within the last 24 hours debugging has broken twice (I can't seem to step in to function calls once I hit a breakpoint). I was able to "fix" this the first time by trying to re-do my setup (ultimately I am not sure what fixed it), but now that it has broken again (and I am not sure what causes this setup to break), I have so far been unable to fix it my repeating my setup process.
**Method 2:** use --extensionDevelopmentPath
This is something that I have just started trying because it seems less than ideal to need to "inline" extensions into vscode via method 1.
The goal of this method is to use `./scripts/code.sh --extensionDevelopmentPath=/Users/nick/code/vscode-go`.
Steps:
- Update vscode-go's package.json
- Remove vscode as a dev dependency from vscode-go
- Remove references to `node ./node_modules/vscode/bin/install`
- Add a refs.d.ts to point to local version of vscode.d.ts
```
    /// <reference path='../../../../src/vs/vscode.d.ts'/>
    /// <reference path='../../../../src/vs/vscode.proposed.d.ts'/> 
    ```
Right now I am stuck on trying to get breakpoints to work with this method. Setting a breakpoint in vscode-go requires vscode-go to be a subdirectory of my project.
I tried creating a sandbox directory that contains just vscode and vscode-go. In this directory I have a .vscode/launch.json that looks like this
```
{
    // Use IntelliSense to learn about possible Node.js debug attributes.
    // Hover to view descriptions of existing attributes.
    // For more information, visit: https://go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?linkid=830387
    "version": "0.2.0",
    "configurations": [
        {
            "type": "node",
            "request": "attach",
            "name": "Attach to Extension Host",
            "protocol": "legacy",
            "port": 5870,
            "sourceMaps": true,
            "outFiles": [
                "/Users/nick/code/sandbox/vscode-clean/out/**/*.js",
                "/Users/nick/code/sandbox/vscode-go/out/**/*.js"
            ]
        }
    ]
}
```
The vscode-go extension appears to load, but my breakpoints in vscode-go files don't catch.

title: How could I create an interactive terminal
body:
I created an extension, which creates a new terminal and executes some shell commands (there are some logic around each commands output, I was using `onDidWriteData` to do that), then users can keep working with terminal.
I notice that the new terminal api finalized, the `onDidWriteData` function is gone. The pseudoterminal seems not able to be interactive, well the terminal doesn't have `onDidWriteData` anymore.
I am not sure if I missed something. Is there an option to turn pseudoterminal to be interactively? Or how can I listen terminal output data (like `onDidWriteData` before) ?

title: Some questions about "user settings"  and "emmet" & "snippet"
body:
- VSCode Version: 1.6.0
- OS Version: windows 10 x64
> I am a Chinese, My English is not very good, these words are written using translation software, Excuse me.
> I like VScode very much，I used it from V0.9 onwards, from the first day I know it, together with a strong recommendation to a friend, although there are still some problems, but I believe it will be getting better and better!
There are a few problems have always wanted to say, but my English is not good, today I have the courage to say it, hoping to improve.
1. user settings：
When we set some options, except the "true" or  "false" option, there is no hint at the value of the comment line, like:
``` json
    // 控制编辑器是否应呈现空白字符
    "editor.renderWhitespace": "none",
    // 控制是否将代码段与其他建议一起显示以及它们的排序方式。
    "editor.snippetSuggestions": "bottom",

--------------------I wish it could be like the following------------
    // 控制编辑器是否应呈现空白字符, valid values: ["none","boundary","all"]
    "editor.renderWhitespace": "none",
    // 控制是否将代码段与其他建议一起显示以及它们的排序方式。valid values: ["top","bottom","inline","none"]
    "editor.snippetSuggestions": "bottom",

This will be very convenient for us to set up
```
1.  "emmet" & "snippet"
In VS code, when we enter the PHP built-in function, like **"echo"**, when we enter "echo", It will appear smart tips, but when we press the `tab` button, it just shows `echo` .
In Sublime Text, when we enter `echo` and press the `tab`, it is `'echo "string";'` , and the cursor on the `string`, it greatly saving times.
``` php
// enter `echo` and press tab:
------------In VS code------------
echo

------------In sublime text------------
echo "string";
```

title: Keeping code navigation in one window only
body:
I use C and typically use two vertical windows, and often navigate back and forth through call graphs by jumping into symbol definitions (F12) and then jump back and forth in the code (ctrl+alt+-/ctrl+shift+-).
What would be really helpful if navigation would occur only in the window it is started. As a consequence, the other window(s) would remain as they were. And often contents of other window(s) have meaning as a context for the work.
So for example, jumping to a definition would happen in the same window instead of switching to one of the other windows.
Regards
Vilho Raatikka

title: What's happened with c-sharp language service?
body:
My [extension](https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items/reflectiondm.ClassyNaming) had a dependency to 'vscode.scharp' language as it is described [here](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-docs/blob/master/docs/extensionAPI/extension-points.md#contributeslanguages). The reason this dependency was introduced was that it was impossible to contribute my own completion suffusions to C# completion list back then. Recently (probably with vscode 0.10.6 update) it suddenly stopped working. A quick investigation shown that the reason is the extension cannot reference 'vscode.csharp' anymore in its extensionDependencies list. I've removed it and extension start working as usual, and still can add its suggestions to c# completion list.
Were there any changes related to csharp language and its interaction with extensions? Everything is working now, but I would really like to know what happened.

title: Bug in file creation and deletion
body:
Hi, the problem is the following: it happens that I have a main.js file which if I modify, the changes are not reflected in the local website or in the command console. Also if I create a copy of the file called main-copy.js and change the path to this file if it works for me, but if I delete the main.js and rename the previous main-copy.js to main.js it will work like the one previously deleted file which I already deleted and cannot modify.
I already tried everything and restarted everything but the bug continues. The only solution is to have another file the same but with a name different from main.js, otherwise it takes the previous file, even though it no longer exists.
